title: Waymo's Driverless Cars Are 'Backsliding' — Blocking Fire Trucks and Ignoring Police
summary: When a fire truck needs to race to a burning building, every second counts. But in San Francisco and Austin, firefighters say they're increasingly blocked by an unexpected obstacle: Waymo's driverless cars. In a private meeting with federal regulators last month, emergency first-responder leaders said the performance of autonomous vehicles is deteriorating. "We are actually seeing something interesting: backsliding of some things that had improved upon," said Mary Ellen Carroll, executive director of San Francisco's Department of Emergency Management. "They are committing more traffic violations." Chief Patrick Rabbitt of the San Francisco Fire Department added, "Waymo is frequently now blocking our fire stations from access. Their default is to freeze." The situation can prevent firetrucks from responding to emergencies in a "timely and appropriate" way, he said. In Austin, Lieutenant William White of the police department said Waymos often fail to recognize or respond to officers' hand signals, leading to cascading delays. "I believe the technology was deployed too quickly in too vast amounts, with hundreds of vehicles, when it wasn't really ready," White said. The complaints come as Waymo expands aggressively. The company now offers driverless rides in parts of 10 US cities, with plans for 10 more this year, including London. It provides 500,000 paid rides weekly — a tenfold increase from last year. Yet these operational hiccups threaten to slow the rollout. In one alarming incident, a Waymo blocked an ambulance for two minutes while it was responding to a downtown Austin shooting that killed three people. Officers reported that connecting with a remote agent can take up to three minutes. During a December power outage in San Francisco, more than 60 Waymos had to be manually retrieved after they froze at intersections without traffic signals. A 911 operator waited on Waymo's hotline for 53 minutes. Waymo spokesperson Julia Ilina said the company values its partnership with first responders and has conducted in-person training for over 35,000 emergency workers. But local officials are pushing for more. New California regulations, effective July, will require autonomous vehicle companies to respond to first-responder calls within 30 seconds and clear vehicles from emergency areas within two minutes. As White put it, "The human element is what's killing them. The moment you introduce the human element, [the vehicles] lack that social awareness of what to do, and they freeze." For now, first responders are left navigating a world where the robots they were promised aren't quite ready for the real world.
why_it_matters: If self-driving cars can't respond properly to emergencies, the safety of entire communities is at risk, especially as these vehicles multiply. This story shows why testing technology in the messy real world is so difficult, and why companies must listen to first responders before lives are lost.

keywords.explanation: When something gets worse again after it had improved.
Cars that drive themselves without a human controlling the steering wheel or pedals.
Government officials who create and enforce rules for industries like transportation.
Put into use or sent out for real-world testing.
A chain reaction of hold-ups where one small wait causes many more.
Physically picked up or moved by a human instead of the car moving on its own.

questions.question: According to the article, what is happening with Waymo's autonomous vehicles?
options: They are getting better at obeying traffic laws.
They are committing more traffic violations than before.
They have stopped operating in San Francisco.
They are being recalled by the government.
questions.correct_answer: They are committing more traffic violations than before.

questions.question: Which city's fire chief said Waymo is 'frequently now blocking our fire stations from access'?
What does Waymo's 'default' behavior lead to, according to the article?
In one alarming incident, how long did a Waymo block an ambulance in Austin?
New California regulations will require autonomous vehicle companies to respond to first-responder calls within how many seconds?
According to Lieutenant White, what is the core problem causing the vehicles to freeze?

background_read: Waymo, a subsidiary of Google's parent company Alphabet, began as Google's self-driving car project in 2009 and launched its first driverless taxi service in Phoenix in 2020. San Francisco's hilly streets, complex intersections, and dense traffic have made it a notoriously difficult testing ground for autonomous vehicles. In 2023, rival robotaxi company Cruise had its permit suspended in California after one of its cars dragged a pedestrian, heightening scrutiny on the entire industry.

The U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration has been investigating multiple incidents involving autonomous vehicles blocking emergency responders. Texas and California each have their own rules for driverless testing, and cities like Austin have become major hubs for companies eager to prove their technology in varied conditions.

First responders rely on siren-and-light priority to reach emergencies within critical time windows; even a two-minute delay can be the difference between life and death. When a robotaxi 'freezes' because it can't read a hand signal, it creates a new kind of traffic hazard that existing road laws never anticipated.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Hook — fire truck seconds count, but Waymo blocks them
└─ specific: San Francisco and Austin first responders' complaints
KEY EVENT: First responders report 'backsliding' in Waymo's behavior at private meeting with regulators
├─ WHO: Mary Ellen Carroll (SF emergency management) & Chief Patrick Rabbitt (SFFD)
├─ WHAT: Blocking fire stations, committing more traffic violations, ignoring hand signals
├─ ONE SIDE: Emergency workers say vehicles freeze, delay response, and are unsafe
└─ OTHER SIDE: Waymo says it values partnership, has trained 35,000 workers, and is expanding
EVIDENCE: Real incidents — blocked ambulance for two minutes in Austin shooting, 60+ Waymos frozen during power outage, 53-minute hotline wait
OPEN QUESTION: Can new regulations (30-second response, two-minute clearance) and better training solve the 'human element' gap?

perspectives.description: Proponents argue that refined autonomous vehicles will eventually reduce human-error car accidents and provide mobility for people who cannot drive. In the long run, they could make roads safer and more efficient.

Critics say deploying the technology too aggressively before it can handle unpredictable human behavior endangers both emergency workers and the public. The reported 'backsliding' suggests the software still struggles with basic real-world judgment.

The fast pace of AI deployment followed by operational hiccups mirrors a pattern seen in many new technologies. Regulators historically struggle to keep up with innovation, leading to a cyclical tightening of rules after public incidents.

New California rules may set a national standard, and improved training with first responders could iron out many issues. However, the industry must still solve the fundamental challenge of giving machines the 'social awareness' to navigate fluid human situations.
